elements.sent-2411-001: The hybridization between light and matter is fundamental for achieving cavity-induced control over quantum materials, necessitating accurate ab initio methods for their analysis.
elements.sent-2411-002: Among these, the quantum electrodynamics Hartree-Fock framework stands out as an essential mean field approximation for electron-electron and electron-photon interactions, forming the basis for advanced post-Hartree-Fock methods like quantum electrodynamics coupled cluster and auxiliary field quantum Monte Carlo.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, trivial quantum electrodynamics Hartere-Fock (QEDHF) methods assume a product state ansatz and thus cannot describe the light-matter Entanglement.
elements.sent-2411-004: Furthermore, our previous work on variational ansatz approaches lacked the capability to capture anharmonic or nonlinear fluctuations, limiting their applicability to strongly coupled systems.
elements.sent-2411-005: To overcome these limitations, we propose an extended QEDHF formalism by introducing a variational Squeeze transformation capable of describing anharmonic quantum fluctuations in photon fields.
elements.sent-2411-006: By optimizing the squeezing parameters, our framework provides a more nuanced and accurate characterization of photon field quantum fluctuations, significantly enhancing the predictive power of QEDHF in strong coupling regimes.
elements.sent-2411-007: Moreover, this formalism enhances the description of light-matter Entanglement, providing a first-principles framework for understanding light-matter hybridization and paving the way for deeper insights into cavity-modified quantum phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-008: Light-matter interactions are a fundamental area of research in quantum chemistry, condensed matter physics, and materials science~\cite{Forn-Diaz:2019vs .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Recent advancements in the strong coupling regime have led to the emergence of new frontiers in chemistry and materials science, such as polariton chemistry~\cite{Hutchison2012ACIE, Ebbesen2016ACR, Xiang:2024vc, Mandal:2023vh, Weight2023pccp and cavity quantum materials~\cite{Schlawin:2022uq, Basov:2017ur, Ashida:2020vh, Hubener:2021wq , which leverage strong light-matter interactions to modify materials and chemical properties.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: These include influencing chemical reactions~\cite{Zhang:2023vo, joel2022nc , energy transfer~\cite{Timmer:2023wn , condensation~\cite{Heinzen:2000uc , quantum phase transitions~\cite{Budden:2021vz, Chiocchetta:2021ut , and quantum information~\cite{Kavokin:2022tn, Ghosh:2020uk .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, the mechanisms underlying light-induced states and emergent properties remain poorly understood.
elements.sent-2411-012: Ab initio methods are crucial for advancing our understanding of these complex systems, including both their thermodynamic and kinetic properties.
elements.sent-2411-013: These methods are also fundamental for nonadiabatic molecular dynamics simulations, which are vital for studying dynamical processes~\cite{Zhang2019JCP, Li2024:sn, Rana2023JPCA, luk_multiscale_2017, groenhof_tracking_2019, Li:2024tp .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The challenges associated with accurately modeling light-matter interactions have stimulated the development of electronic structure theories for quantum electrodynamics (QED) problems, including QED density functional theory (QEDFT)~\cite{Ruggenthaler2014PRA, Tokatly2013PRL, Flick2017PNAS , variational reduced density matrix methods~\cite{Matousek:2024vi , and various wavefunction-based techniques.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Among the wavefunction-based methods, the quantum electrodynamics Hartree-Fock (QEDHF) framework~\cite{haugland_coupled_2020, foley:2023CPR, Riso:2022uw, Zhang:2023vt, Cui:2024ul stands out as a foundational mean-field theory, providing an essential first step for any first-principles approach.}
elements.sent-2411-016: As a mean-field theory, QEDHF not only allows for a tractable approximation of the electron-photon interaction but also serves as the basis for more sophisticated post-Hartree-Fock (post-HF) methods, such as QED-coupled cluster (QED-CC)~\cite{haugland_coupled_2020, white:2020jcp, Weight2023pccp, PRR023262, yang:2024be , Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG)~\cite{Matousek:2024vi, Shaffer:2024et, Passetti:2023tc , and quantum Monte Carlo (QMC)~\cite{Weight:2024vi, Weight:2024af .}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Therefore, a robust QEDHF reference is critical, as it directly influences the accuracy and efficiency of these higher-order methods.
elements.sent-2411-018: In our previous work, we developed a variational displacement transformation based QEDHF framework, namely VT-QEDHF~\cite{Zhang:2023vt , enabling the treatment of light-matter interactions across all coupling regimes.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The variational displaced QEDHF formalism effectively decouples the electron and photon degrees of freedom by shifting the photon field, providing a versatile tool for studying polaritonic states.
elements.sent-2411-020: However, the primary limitation of this approach lies in its focus on harmonic fluctuations, which, while adequate at lower coupling strengths, becomes insufficient as the coupling regime intensifies and anharmonic effects become significant.
elements.sent-2411-021: To address this challenge, we introduce an extension to the QEDHF formalism by incorporating a variational squeeze (VSQ) transformation~\cite{Feinberg:1990sp, Trapper:1994tg, Barone:2008ug, Walls:1983tv .}
elements.sent-2411-022: The Squeeze transformation, widely utilized in quantum optics to describe states with modified uncertainty relations, captures the anharmonic fluctuations that arise in strongly coupled light-matter systems.
elements.sent-2411-023: Treating the squeezing parameters as variational allows us to optimize the system's energy, leading to a more accurate and comprehensive description of quantum fluctuations in the photon field.
elements.sent-2411-024: Furthermore, this scheme can quantitatively estimate light-matter Entanglement, as demonstrated by benchmarking against Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) calculations.
elements.sent-2411-025: Since Entanglement is an important indicator of polariton-induced properties, our approach provides significant insights into the interplay between electronic and photonic degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-026: This advancement is expected to have substantial implications for the design and control of quantum materials and devices.

elements.sent-2411-131: This analysis highlights the relationship between VSQ-QEDHF and VT-QEDHF: VT-QEDHF corresponds to the non-squeezing limit of the VSQ-QEDHF method.
elements.sent-2411-132: The introduction of the squeeze operator captures additional nonlinear effects, further lowering the system's energy.
elements.sent-2411-133: This demonstrates the necessity of including the squeeze operator for accurately describing light-matter interactions, particularly at strong coupling.

elements.sent-2411-134: As discussed above, the introduction of the squeeze operator incorporates a nonlinear, anharmonic effect on the photon field due to electron-photon interactions.
elements.sent-2411-135: This nonlinear behavior becomes increasingly significant as the coupling strength grows, particularly in the strong and ultrastrong coupling regimes.
elements.sent-2411-136: The squeeze operator modifies the photon states, leading to deformations in the vacuum state of the field that reflect a redistribution of quantum fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-137: These fluctuations can no longer be described by a simple harmonic oscillator model, as they acquire a squeezed nature, altering both the amplitude and phase quadratures of the photon field.
elements.sent-2411-138: Figure~\ref{fig:e_vs_f b shows the energy difference between the VSQ-QEDHF and VT-QEDHF methods as a function of the coupling strength.}
elements.sent-2411-139: The results indicate that the energy difference increases monotonically with increasing coupling strength, highlighting the growing importance of the squeeze operator in accurately capturing photon-mediated effects in this regime.
elements.sent-2411-140: At larger coupling strengths, the electron-photon interactions become so strong that the bare harmonic approximation (as employed in VT-QEDHF) fails to fully describe the system's quantum flutuation.
elements.sent-2411-141: The squeeze operator introduces critical corrections by accounting for photon number fluctuations and the resulting anharmonicity.
elements.sent-2411-142: This correction is particularly significant because, in the ultrastrong coupling limit, these fluctuations influence both the ground and excited states of the system, leading to non-negligible energy shifts.
elements.sent-2411-143: By incorporating the squeeze operator, the VSQ-QEDHF method effectively captures these shifts, providing a more accurate description of the photon field and its coupling to the matter system.
elements.sent-2411-144: Therefore, the squeezing effect is not a minor correction; it becomes an essential component in the ultrastrong coupling regime.
elements.sent-2411-145: Neglecting it would result in substantial inaccuracies in both energy estimations and the characterization of photon-matter Entanglement.

The entropy difference increases with increasing coupling strength, emphasizing the importance of the squeezing operator in the strong coupling regime. In panels (a) and (b), the frequency and coupling strength are fixed at 0.05 and 0.1, respectively. The inset highlights the entropy difference between the VSQ-QEDHF and VT-QEDHF methods, which becomes more pronounced as the frequency decreases. Similarly, as shown in a), the difference grows with increasing coupling strength. } \label{fig:entropy_vs_w_lambda}
elements.sent-2411-146: Finally, we discuss the entropy and light-matter entanglement, which are essential for examining the quantum effects of cavity photons on electronic structures.
elements.sent-2411-147: As mentioned earlier, the trivial QEDHF ansatz assumes a simple product form, inherently failing to describe light-matter Entanglement.
elements.sent-2411-148: Consequently, the Von Neumann entropy is always zero, regardless of the frequency or coupling strength.
elements.sent-2411-149: In contrast, both VT-QEDHF and VSQ-QEDHF employ entangled ans\"atze, providing valuable insights into light-matter Entanglement across the parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-150: Consistent with the ground-state energy calculations, VSQ-QEDHF captures more electron-photon correlations, resulting in a more accurate estimation of light-matter Entanglement.
elements.sent-2411-151: Figure~\ref{fig:entropy_vs_w_lambda illustrates the entropy as a function of cavity frequency $\omega$ and coupling strength $\lambda$.}
elements.sent-2411-152: As shown in the figure, the entropy increases monotonically with increasing coupling strength, reflecting the strengthening of light-matter correlations.
elements.sent-2411-153: However, the relationship with frequency is more intricate.
elements.sent-2411-154: The entropy initially decreases with increasing frequency, reaches a minimum, then begins to rise, eventually saturating at higher frequency limits.
elements.sent-2411-155: This non-monotonic behavior highlights the delicate interplay between the photonic and electronic DOFs under varying frequency regimes.
elements.sent-2411-156: In summary, this work introduces a significant extension of the QEDHF method through the variational squeezing ansatz.
elements.sent-2411-157: The QEDHF framework is a foundational mean-field theory that approximates electron-photon interactions.
elements.sent-2411-158: However, the conventional QEDHF method assumes a simple product state, limiting its ability to describe light-matter Entanglement.
elements.sent-2411-159: To address this limitation, we propose a Variational Squeeze (VSQ) transformation within the QEDHF framework.
elements.sent-2411-160: This extended formalism incorporates anharmonic fluctuations to more accurately capture the quantum behavior of the photon field, particularly in the strong coupling regime.
elements.sent-2411-161: By optimizing the squeezing parameters, the VSQ-QEDHF method provides a more comprehensive description of light-matter interactions, improving the predictive accuracy of QEDHF.
elements.sent-2411-162: We demonstrated the efficacy of the VSQ-QEDHF method using a one-dimensional Hubbard-Holstein-type model and realistic molecular systems.
elements.sent-2411-163: The results show that the new approach better captures quantum fluctuations, leading to improved energy calculations.
elements.sent-2411-164: Additionally, the VSQ-QEDHF method effectively describes light-matter Entanglement, a capability lacking in traditional QEDHF methods.
elements.sent-2411-165: Our benchmark against DMRG indicates that the VSQ-QEDHF method exhibits qualitative agreement with DMRG results for entropy (light-matter Entanglement) as a function of coupling strength, photon frequency, and correlation strength.
elements.sent-2411-166: We observe that light-matter Entanglement decreases with increasing photon frequency.
elements.sent-2411-167: In terms of correlation strength, Entanglement initially increases, followed by a decrease as correlation-induced localization dominates at larger correlation limits.
elements.sent-2411-168: This nuanced behavior underscores the versatility of the VSQ-QEDHF method in capturing complex light-matter dynamics across varying interaction strengths.
elements.sent-2411-169: The integration of the VSQ-QEDHF method with post-HF techniques holds great promise.
elements.sent-2411-170: This framework could accelerate the convergence of more advanced methods, such as QED-CC~\cite{haugland_coupled_2020, white:2020jcp, Weight2023pccp, PRR023262, yang:2024be and the QED counterparts of Auxiliary Field or Diffusion Quantum Monte Carlo (AFQMC)~\cite{Weight:2024vi, Weber:2024af , a project currently underway within our team.}}
elements.sent-2411-171: By incorporating variational squeezing, these post-HF methods are expected to benefit from more accurate initial conditions, enabling faster and more efficient simulations of complex light-matter systems.
elements.sent-2411-172: The ability to better account for anharmonic photon fluctuations and light-matter Entanglement should make these approaches more powerful and applicable across a wider range of coupling regimes, paving the way for precise explorations of quantum materials under strong light-matter coupling conditions.

elements.section-2411-005: Properties of displacement and squeeze operators
elements.sent-2411-175: Before deriving the detailed equations, we discuss the properties and physical meanings of the displacement and squeeze operators.
elements.sent-2411-176: The squeeze operator is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:squeeze_op} \hat{S} ( F_{\alpha} ) = \prod_{\alpha} exp \left[ \frac{1}{2} \left(F^*_{\alpha} \hat{b}^2_\alpha - F_{\alpha}\hat{b}^{\dag 2}_\alpha \right) \right],\end{equation} where $F_{\alpha \equiv r_{\alpha e^{i\theta_\alpha}$ is generally complex.}}
elements.sent-2411-177: The squeeze operator is a unitary operator, satisfying $\hat{S (F)\hat{S ^\dag(F)=\hat{S ^\dag(F)\hat{S (F)=\hat{I}$, where $\hat{I}$ is the identity operator.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-178: The squeeze operator can be applied to any state.
elements.sent-2411-179: For instance, its action on the vacuum (or coherent) state produces a squeezed (or squeezed coherent) state.
elements.sent-2411-180: Squeezed states are widely utilized in precision measurements, such as interferometric measurements in the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory (LIGO)~\cite{Bailes:2021tz .}
elements.sent-2411-181: Due to the presence of ladder operators within the exponential, the action of the squeeze operator on individual bosonic creation or annihilation operators introduces a mixture of both creation and annihilation operators, \begin{equation}\hat{S}^{\dag} (F_{\alpha}) \hat{b}^{\dag}_{\alpha} \hat{S} (F_{\alpha}) = \cosh(r_{\alpha}) \hat{b}^{\dag}_{\alpha} - \sinh(r_{\alpha}) \hat{b}_{\alpha}, \hat{S}^{\dag} (F_{\alpha}) \hat{b}_{\alpha} \hat{S} (F_{\alpha}) = \cosh(r_{\alpha}) \hat{b}_{\alpha} - \sinh(r_{\alpha}) \hat{b}^{\dag}_{\alpha}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-182: The displacement operator in quantum optics is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:cs_op} \hat{D} ( z_{\alpha} ) = \prod_{\alpha} exp \left[ - \left(z^*_{\alpha} \hat{b}_{\alpha} - z_{\alpha}\hat{b}^{\dag}_{\alpha} \right) \right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-183: Specifically, when $z_\alpha$ is a scalar, the action of the displacement operator on the vacuum state produces a coherent state $\ket{z_\alpha}$.
elements.sent-2411-184: The bosonic operators in the coherent state representation become, \begin{equation}\label{eq:displacez} \hat{D}^{\dag} (z_{\alpha}) \hat{b}^{\dag}_{\alpha} \hat{D} (z_{\alpha}) = \hat{b}^{\dag}_{\alpha} - z^*_{\alpha}, \hat{D}^{\dag} (z_{\alpha}) \hat{b}_{\alpha} \hat{D} (z_{\alpha}) = \hat{b}_{\alpha} - z_{\alpha}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-185: In the study of electron-boson interactions, it is often beneficial to introduce an electronic operator $\hat{f}$ in the displacement operator to account for orbital relaxation effects.
elements.sent-2411-186: The generalized displacement operator is then defined as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:vt_op} \hat{D} ( \hat{f}_{\alpha} ) = \prod_{\alpha} exp \left[ -\hat{f}_{\alpha} \left( \hat{b}_{\alpha} - \hat{b}^{\dag}_{\alpha} \right) \right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-187: In principle, there are no restrictions on the choice of the $\hat{f}$ operator.
elements.sent-2411-188: For an electron-boson interacting system with an interaction Hamiltonian of the form $\hat{H _{\text{int = \sum_\alpha \hat{O ^\alpha_f \hat{O ^\alpha_b$, where $\hat{O _f$ and $\hat{O _b$ are fermionic and bosonic operators respectively, a natural choice is $\hat{f = \hat{O _f$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-189: This choice partially decouples the electron-boson interaction through the (variational) Lang-Firsov transformation~\cite{Zhang:2023vt, Zhang:2015ua .}
elements.sent-2411-190: For light-matter interactions considered in this work, $\hat{f _\alpha = \frac{f_\alpha \boldsymbol{\lambda _\alpha \cdot \boldsymbol{D {\sqrt{2\omega_\alpha }$, where $f_\alpha$ is a variational parameter controlling the strength of displacement.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-191: Similar to the coherent state displacement, the similarity transformation with $\hat{D (\hat{f )$ modifies the bosonic operator as \begin{equation}\hat{D}^\dag(\hat{f}_\alpha) b^\dag_\alpha \hat{D}(z) = b^\dag - \hat{f}_\alpha.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-192: If the orbital relaxation effects are neglected and the electronic DOF in $f_\alpha$ is traced out, $\hat{D (\hat{f )$ reduces to the coherent state displacement $\hat{D (z_\alpha)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: However, the introduction of the electronic operator $\hat{f}$ grants $\hat{D (\hat{f )$ additional variational flexibility.}}
elements.sent-2411-194: Notably, $\hat{D (\hat{f )$ introduces orbital-dependent displacement to the electronic operator, whereas the coherent state displacement $\hat{D (z_\alpha)$ does not affect electronic operators.}}}

elements.subsection-2411-007: QEDHF formalism with the Variational Gaussian Squeezed State (GSS)
elements.sent-2411-232: This section presents the detailed derivation of the QEDHF formalism within the Gaussian Squeezed State (GSS), where the wavefunction ansatz is defined as \begin{equation}\hat{U}(\hat{f}_\alpha, F) = \hat{D}(\hat{f}_\alpha) \hat{S}(F).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-233: The action of this transformation on bosonic and electronic operators is as follows \begin{equation}\hat{U}^\dag \hat{b}^\dag \hat{U} = \hat{S}^\dag(r)[\hat{b}^\dag - z_\alpha] \hat{S}(r) = \cosh(r) \hat{b}^\dag - \sinh(r) \hat{b} - z_\alpha. \hat{U}^\dag [\hat{b}^\dag + \hat{b}] \hat{U} = \hat{S}^\dag(r)[\hat{b}^\dag + \hat{b} - z_\alpha] \hat{S}(r),\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\hat{U}^\dag \hat{c}_i \hat{U} = \sum_j \hat{c}_j \hat{X}_{ij},\end{equation} where $\hat{\mathcal{X _{ij}$ is given by \begin{equation}\hat{X}_{ij} = \hat{S}^\dag(r)\left[ e^{-\frac{f_\alpha\lambda\cdotD}{\sqrt{2\omega}}\left(\hb^\dag_\alpha - \hb_\alpha\right)} \Big|_{ij}\right]\hat{S}(r) \nonumber = \exp\left[-\frac{f_\alpha\lambda\cdotD}{\sqrt{2\omega}}\left(\hb^\dag_\alpha - \hb_\alpha\right)\left[\cosh(r)+\sinh(r)\right) \right]\Big|_{ij} \nonumber = \exp\left[-\frac{f_\alpha\lambda\cdotD}{\sqrt{2\omega}}\left(\hb^\dag_\alpha - \hb_\alpha\right)e^{r_\alpha} \right]\Big|_{ij}.\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-237: Like the VT-QEDHF case, the $\hat{\mathcal{X }$ operators introduce the Franck-Condon factors to the one-body and two-body integrals.}

elements.subsection-2411-008: QEDHF with Variational Squeezed Gaussian State (SGS)

elements.sent-2411-249: After the transformation, the electronic Hamiltonian remains unaffected by the squeeze operator.
elements.sent-2411-250: Only the bilinear coupling and bosonic Hamiltonians are altered.
elements.sent-2411-251: In the limit of a single bosonic Fock state (vacuum state), the bilinear term vanishes, and the optimization of the squeezed bosonic term becomes identical to the bare one.
elements.sent-2411-252: Consequently, the squeezing operator has no effect in the single Fock state limit.
elements.subsection-2411-009: QEDHF Formalism with Squeezed Coherent State (SCS)
elements.sent-2411-253: The squeezed coherent state (SCS) is defined as \begin{equation}\ket{F, z} = \hat{U}\ket{0_p}= \hat{S}(F)\hat{D}(z)\ket{0_p}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-254: The effective Hamiltonian in the squeezed coherent state (SCS) representation becomes, \begin{equation}\hat{H}^e_{scs} = \hat{D}^\dag(z)\left[\hat{S}^\dag(F)\hat{H}_{PF}\hat{S}(F)\right]\hat{D}(z) \equiv \hat{D}^\dag(z)\hat{H}_{S}\hat{D}(z).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-255: Since both $\hat{D (z)$ and $\hat{S (F)$ act only on the bosonic operator, the SCS transformation affects only the photonic part of the Hamiltonian.}}

elements.sent-2411-259: Compared to the coherent state representation, the SCS transformation modifies only the photon Hamiltonian, as shown in Eq.~\ref{eq:photonham , and boosts the DSE-mediated one-body integrals as \begin{equation}h^{DSE}_{pq} = -\frac{1}{2}Q^\alpha_{pq} - e^{-r} \sqrt{\frac{\omega_\alpha}{2}}g_\alpha + \omega_\alpha \frac{z^2_\alpha}{N_e}\left[\cosh(2r) - 2\sinh(2r)\right] S_{pq},\end{equation} which is formally similar to the original DSE-mediated one electron integral in the CS representation in Eq.~\ref{eq:hamcs .}}
elements.sent-2411-260: The Coherent Squeeze State (CSS) is defined as \begin{equation}\hat{U}(z,F) = \hat{D}(z)\hat{S}(F)\ket{0}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-261: Since $\hat{D (z)$ and $\hat{S (F)$ do not commute, the results from the Coherent Squeeze State and Squeezed Coherent State differ.}}
elements.sent-2411-262: The effective Hamiltonian in the CSS representation can be obtained from Eq.~\ref{eq:hamcs by replacing $\hb_\alpha$ with $\cosh(r)\hb_\alpha - \sinh(r)\hb^\dag_\alpha$, \begin{equation}\hat{H}_{CSS} = \hat{H}_e + \frac{1}{2}(\ldotd)^2 +\sqrt{\frac{\omega_\alpha}{2}}e^{-r}[\lambda_\alpha\cdot(D-\langleD\rangle)](\hb^\dag_\alpha+\hb_\alpha) + \frac{1}{2}\left[\lambda_\alpha\cdot(D-\langleD\rangle)\right]^2 \nonumber + \omega_\alpha \left\{\cosh(2r)(\hb^\dag_\alpha \hb_\alpha + \frac{1}{2}) -\sinh(2r)(\hb^{\dag 2}_\alpha + \hb^2_\alpha) \right\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-263: The CSS transformation renormalizes only the bilinear coupling term and the photon Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-264: In the limit of a single-photon basis set (vacuum state), the bilinear term vanishes, and it is straightforward to verify that the squeezed state does not affect the ground state energy.
elements.sent-2411-265: However, in the many-photon basis set or the complete photon basis set limit, the bilinear term no longer vanishes, and the squeeze operation will impact the energies.
elements.sent-2411-266: The wavefunction in the original basis set is \begin{equation}\ket{\Phi} = \hat{U}\ket{\text{HF}}\otimes \ket{0_p}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-267: From the mean-field solution to $\hat{\mathcal{H _{GSS}$, we obtain the $\ket{\text{HF }$ wavefunction and the parameters $f_\alpha$ and $r_\alpha$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-271: The photon density matrix can then be computed via the partial trace over the electronic DOF \begin{equation}\rho_{ph} = \text{Tr}_e[\rho] = \sum_\mu |C_\mu|^2 \ket{Z_\mu, F}\bra{Z_\mu, F}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-272: The corresponding Von Neumann entropy is \begin{equation}S_{ph} = - K_B \text{Tr}\left[\rho_{ph}\ln(\rho_{ph})\right],\end{equation} which measures the light-matter entanglement.. \end{widetext.}
